coredata.dc:title: In-situ one-step synthesis of porous monolayer carbon nitride nanosheets doped with carbon quantum dots for photocatalytic degradation of Meloxicam
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yongqiang
Zhang, Mengdan
Zhao, Jiamei
Chen, Chen
Zhou, Yingying
Zheng, Xin
Zhang, Conglu
coredata.dc:description: The energy storage and upconversion effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could improve the photocatalytic efficiency of materials. In this study, a visible-light-driven photocatalyst, CQDs-doped carbon nitride nanosheets composite (CNS/CQDs) was in-situ synthesized by the thermal stripping of carbon nitride bulk (CNB). The as-prepared CNS/CQDs photocatalyst was systematically characterized and applied to the removal of Meloxicam (Mel) in water. The results showed that the introduction of CQDs could improve the visible light utilization of the carbon nitride. Mel (50 mL, 10 mg L−1) was degraded by 99.60% in 45 min with CNS/CQDs (20 mg). The stability and recycling experiments showed that CNS/CQDs could maintain high photocatalytic activity under complex matrix and multiple cycles. This work will provide a new attempt to synthesize the CQDs doped photocatalytic materials for the effective removal of organic pollutants in water under visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
Carbon quantum dots
In-situ one-step synthesis
Photocatalytic degradation
Meloxicam

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Liaoning Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Fund Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province, China(LJKZ0910) and the Career Development Support Plan for Young and Middle −aged Teachers in Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, China (ZQN2021026). The author thanks Dr. Guo Dong (Beijing Normal University) for his help in characterization analysis.

This work was supported by the Scientific Research Fund Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province , China( LJKZ0910 ) and the Career Development Support Plan for Young and Middle −aged Teachers in Shenyang Pharmaceutical University , China ( ZQN2021026 ). The author thanks Dr. Guo Dong (Beijing Normal University) for his help in characterization analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CNS/CQDs. Insert image: (a) CNB, (b) CNS-air-500 ℃− 120 min, (c) CNS-N2-500 ℃− 120 min, (d) CNS-air-580 ℃− 60 min and (e) CNS/CQDs.

(A) Full XRD measurements and (B) partial enlarged view of: (a) CNB, (b) CNS-air-500 ℃− 120 min, (c) CNS-N2-500 ℃− 120 min, (d) CNS-air-580 ℃− 60 min and (e) CNS/CQDs； (C and D) Size & Strain Plot of as-synthesized CNB and CNS/CQDs, respectively.
XPS survey spectra: (A) CNS/CQDs and (B) CNB; XPS high resolution spectra: O 1 s of (C) CNS/CQDs and (D) CNB, N 1 s of (E) CNS/CQDs and (F) CNB, C 1 s of (G) CNS/CQDs and (H) CNB.
SEM images of (A-B) CNB and (C-D) CNS/CQDs.
TEM images of (A-C) CNB and (D-F) CNS/CQDs.
AFM images of the (A) 3D and (B) 2D topographic structure about CNS/CQDs; the height curve determined along the line between (C) blue and (D) red.
(A) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms; (B) pore size distribution for the synthesized materials; (C) cumulative pore volume and (D) cumulative surface area of: CNB and CNS/CQDs.

(A) UV-Vis DRS and (B) the corresponding band-gap plots of: (a) CNB, (b) CNS-air-500 ℃− 120 min, (c) CNS-N2-500 ℃− 120 min, (d) CNS-air-580 ℃− 60 min and (e) CNS/CQDs; (C) VB XPS plots; (D) electronic band structure diagram.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of: (a) CNB, (b) CNS-air-500 ℃− 120 min, (c) CNS-N2-500 ℃− 120 min, (d) CNS-air-580 ℃− 60 min and (e) CNS/CQDs; (B) time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of the CNB, CNS and CNS/CQDs; (C) upconversion photoluminescent spectra of CNS/CQDs; (D) upconversion photoluminescent spectra of CNB and CNS/CQDs under 800 nm femtosecond pulse laser excitation.
Degradation of Mel under different conditions: (A) different materials; (B) pH; (C) Mel concentration; (D) catalyst amount.
(A) Effect of different water matrices on the degradation of Mel; (B) recyclability of CNS/CQDs; (C) changes of TOC removal rate; (D) capture experiment of active substances.
Schematic illustration of Mel photocatalytic degradation over CNS/CQDs.
CNS/CQDs on Mel degradation dynamics parameters.
CNS/CQDs for Mel degradation dynamics parameters: degradation rate constant and half-life at the first-order reaction kinetics.

head.ce:title: In-situ one-step synthesis of porous monolayer carbon nitride nanosheets doped with carbon quantum dots for photocatalytic degradation of Meloxicam

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (Benxi), Liaoning Province 117004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (Benxi)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (Benxi), Liaoning Province 117004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The energy storage and upconversion effect of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could improve the photocatalytic efficiency of materials. In this study, a visible-light-driven photocatalyst, CQDs-doped carbon nitride nanosheets composite (CNS/CQDs) was in-situ synthesized by the thermal stripping of carbon nitride bulk (CNB). The as-prepared CNS/CQDs photocatalyst was systematically characterized and applied to the removal of Meloxicam (Mel) in water. The results showed that the introduction of CQDs could improve the visible light utilization of the carbon nitride. Mel (50 mL, 10 mg L−1) was degraded by 99.60% in 45 min with CNS/CQDs (20 mg). The stability and recycling experiments showed that CNS/CQDs could maintain high photocatalytic activity under complex matrix and multiple cycles. This work will provide a new attempt to synthesize the CQDs doped photocatalytic materials for the effective removal of organic pollutants in water under visible light.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CNS/CQDs were synthesized in one step in situ, the characteristics of CQDs improved the utilization rate of sunlight by CNS/CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphite phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4)，as a typical two-dimensional structure polymer semiconductor has been widely used in hydrogen production, organic synthesis and degradation of pollutants due to its acid and alkali resistance and narrow energy band (2.7 eV) [1–3]. In the photocatalysis process, because of the layered accumulation of carbon nitride bulk (CNB), photoelectrons (e-) can be preferentially trapped by holes (h+). In addition, the band gap width of g-C3N4 not only affects its visible light response range, but also determines the redox ability to the targets [4]. Zhang et al. [5,6] reported that the recombination of electron-hole pairs (e--h+) could be efficiently reduced by adjusting the structure of carbon nitride to form a sheet-like two-dimensional material. However, compared with CNB, the two-dimensional carbon nitride nanosheets (CNS) have the expansion of the band gap, as a result, the application is always influenced [7,8]. Therefore, how to improve the visible light utilization rate of CNS has become the focus of research.

Recent studies have shown that the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can change the electronic structure and store the energy to promote the transfer of charge carriers [9,10]. In addition, the upconversion characteristics of CQDs can also cause the adjustment of the intrinsic electron and energy band structure, resulting in the enhancement of visible light utilization of materials [11,12]. Traditional composite materials of carbon nitride and CQDs are generally synthesized by ex-situ method [13]. However, this method has the disadvantages of high cost, cumbersome steps, and environmental unfriendliness. It is necessary to develop a simple and convenient method to synthesize the carbon nitride and CQDs composites.

Based on the above considerations, an in-situ one-step method was proposed for preparing the CNS/CQDs. Meloxicam (Mel), as a representative enol non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, with biological toxicity and recalcitrance to microbial degradation was used as the target to evaluate the photocatalytic properties of CNS/CQDs. The thermal stripping conditions of CNS synthesis were investigated. The crystal structure, morphological composition, optical properties and photocatalytic activity of the CNS/CQDs were studied. The results show that the photocatalytic performance of CNS/CQDs is far superior to CNB and ordinary CNS.

Dicyandiamide (Dicy, 99.0%) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Meloxicam (Mel, 99.0%) was purchased from Shanghai Macleans Biochemical Company. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≧ 96.0%) was purchased from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0 ~ 38.0%) was purchased from Li'an Long Bohua Pharmaceutical Chemical Co., Ltd. Benzoquinone (BQ, 98.0%) was purchased from Shanghai Xianding Biological Co., Ltd. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na, 99.0%) was purchased from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, ≧ 99.5%) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Deionized water (DI) was used throughout the experiment.

CNB powder was synthesized according to the previous literature [14]. In short, Dicyandiamide (4 g) was placed in an alumina porcelain cabinet, and then heated at 550 °C (heating rate of 2.3 °C min−1) in air atmosphere for 4 h in a horizontal tube furnace (OTF-1200-X-S). After cooling to room temperature, the resulting yellow agglomerates were ground into powder in a mortar.

CNS/CQDs material was prepared in situ by the thermal oxidative stripping of CNB. Specifically, CNB (170 mg) was placed in the porcelain ark and heated at 580 °C (heating rate of 5.0 °C min−1) for 2 h in an air atmosphere in a horizontal tube furnace. Finally, the white powders of CNS/CQDs were obtained with a yield of about 20.0%. Compared with the synthesis of CNS in the literature, the yield is increased by 14.0% [15]. For comparison, the thermal stripping conditions of material, such as different ambient atmosphere, temperature and time were investigated. The naming method of different products obtained is as follows: for example, CNS-air-500 ℃− 120 min represents that in the air atmosphere, the stripping temperature is 500 ℃ and the stripping time is 120 min. The preparation process of CNS/CQDs was shown in Fig. 1 . The actual colors of the CNB, CNS and CNS/CQDs samples were presented in the insert figure of Fig. 1. The pyrolysis of CNB could change the binding sites of the "C" and "N" bonds. Under different thermal stripping conditions, the hydrogen bond formed by van der Waals force in CNB will be broken [15]. In addition, the reduction in hydrogen bonding leads to an increase in the exposure of the "N" site. In an air atmosphere, "O" and "C" atoms combine to form a porous material, and further form CNS/CQDs.

The crystal characteristics of the sample were analyzed by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD, Ultima IV, 2θ range 10–90°, scanning step 5°). The micro morphologies of the sample were obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Gemini 300) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai F 30). The thickness of the sample was measured by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Nanoscope V Multimode 8). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha) was used to analyze the element composition, content and valence information of the sample. Zeta potential (ζ) was obtained by Zeta analyzer (ζ, ZS 90). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured on an Autosorb-iQ analyzer at 77.35 K. The change of the energy band structure of the sample was obtained by ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-Vis DRS, Lambda 750). The photoluminescence spectra of the sample ware analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer (FLS 1000).

The photodegradation of Mel was carried out at room temperature with a 500 W xenon lamp (PL-XQ500W) as a visible light source. Specifically, Material (20 mg) was added to the Mel solution (50 mL, 10 mg L−1). After 30 min of dark adsorption, the solution was placed under the xenon lamp for photocatalytic degradation. The light with wavelength less than 420 nm was removed by a cut-off filter, and the distance between suspension surface and light source was about 15 cm. At specific time intervals, an aliquot was extracted and filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane filters to remove the photocatalyst. The Mel concentrations were determined by UV Probe 2450 spectroscopy at a wavelength of 360 nm. The total organic carbon (TOC) values were analyzed by the total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-L CPN CN200).

The crystal structure of the synthesized materials was studied by XRD measurements and shown in Fig. 2. The prepared CNB, CNS and CNS/CQDs show the obvious and similar diffraction peaks (Fig. 2 A). In the diffraction peaks of all samples, the weak peak at 13.10° is assigned to the (100) in-plane structure stacking motif of the tri-s-triazine unit [16]. CNB shows a strong diffraction peak at 27.52°, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane stacking of the conjugated aromatic hydrocarbon system [17]. However, for CNS, especially CNS/CQDs, the peak value moves slightly to 27.68° and finally to 27.92° (Fig. 2B), indicating that the corridor distance between the basic slices in the slice structure is reduced. According to the literature [18], the flakes in CNB may be undulating, but they can be flattened by further heating, resulting in the denser accumulation and a reduction in corridor distance. In addition, the characteristic peak intensities of CNS and CNS/CQDs after thermal stripping are weaker than that of CNB, which means the crystallinities of the materials decrease [19,20]. Significantly, a new peak at 26.19° is found in CNS/CQDs (Fig. 2B), corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of typical carbon (PDF, #75–0444). Compared with pure carbon, the peak shifts slightly to the lower angle, which may be caused by the peak superposition of the (111) plane of CQDs and the (002) plane of g-C3N4. According to the Bragg equation, the reduction of the interplanar spacing and the increase of the interaction between the π-π-stacked large aromatic supramolecular layers are the fundamental factors of this phenomenon [21]. However, this peak does not exist in the CNS obtained under other thermal stripping conditions, indicating that there is no CQDs generation. The characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 and carbon are retained in the diffraction peaks of CNS/CQDs. According to Scherrer Equation, the grain sizes of CNB and CNS/CQDs before and after stripping are calculated to be about 7.4 nm and 0.9 nm. The microscopic strain is about 0.00% and 17.09%, respectively (Fig. 2C-D).

The full XPS spectra of CNS/CQDs and CNB are illustrated in Fig. 3A-B, which confirms the presence of C, N and O. The O 1 s spectra of CNS/CQDs (Fig. 3C) can be deconvoluted into two peaks located at 532.68 and 533.94 eV, which correspond to C-O and CO, respectively [22,23]. In O 1 s spectra of CNB (Fig. 3D), the peak at 532.81 eV is related to the oxygen in the OH- group. The corresponding N 1 s spectra of CNS/CQDs (Fig. 3E) can be fitted to four peaks at 399.23, 400.46, 401.64 and 405.09 eV, which are attributed to -CN-C-, -N-C3, -C-N-H and π-excitation，respectively. It is consistent with the literature report [24]. CNB also fits the same peak, as shown in Fig. 3F. There are six peaks at 284.80, 285.43, 286.19, 288.51, 289.38 and 294.15 eV in C1s spectra of CNS/CQDs (Fig. 3G), which correspond to C-C, -C-NHx (x = 1, 2), C-O, -NC-(N2)-, CO, and π-excitation，respectively [25]. The same peak is also observed in CNB, but with a slight change (Fig. 3H). The slight shifts of the C 1 s and N 1 s peaks indicate that the morphology and microstructure of the photocatalyst have changed [26]. The element content and C/N ratio were determined by elemental analysis, as shown in Table S1. In CNS/CQDs, the average C/N atomic ratio is 1.084 and the oxygen content is 2.95%, which are higher than those in CNB (C/N = 0.892, Atomic (O 1 s) = 2.71%). This result further confirms the occurrence of terminal amino group oxidation. During the stripping process, not only C-O and CO are formed, but also the elemental carbon appears in the form of CQDs in the lamellar structure of g-C3N4.

The morphologies and microstructures of CNB and CNS/CQDs were obtained by SEM ( Fig. 4). CNB exhibits a smooth layered stacked structure (Fig. 4A-B). Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D prove that the surface of CNS/CQDs is porous, which is attributed to the etching characteristics of air. At the same time, the volume of CNS/CQDs is much larger than that of CNB (Fig. S1), indicating that CNS/CQDs are in a fluffy state. In addition, Fig. 4D confirms that the basic unit of CNS/CQDs is a single-layer nanosheet with lateral dimensions ranging from submicron to several microns. The curvature of the substrate minimizes the surface energy and the edges are jagged, which will help to enhance the photocatalytic performance [27].

TEM further explores the detailed microstructures of CNB and CNS/CQDs ( Fig. 5). As shown in Fig. 5A-B, CNB has a large number of stacked block structures. Fig. 5C further shows stacked nanosheets with short-order aggregation morphology of CNB. In contrast, CNS/CQDs display velvety nanostructures with highly transparent features, wrinkled and corrugated edges (Fig. 5D-F). The reason is that the polymerized melon unit chains in the CNB layer will gradually oxidize and decompose during the stripping process. Moreover, from Fig. 5E, it can be clearly observed that CQDs are spherical particles with a lateral scale about 20 nm and evenly distributed on the surface of CNS/CQDs. The initial thermal stripping temperature of CNB was measured by a thermos-gravimetric analyzer (Fig. S2A). The formation process of CNS/CQDs was clarified by yield measurement (Fig. S2B-C) and TEM analysis (Figs. S3-S5). Taking the stripping time as the example, the weight of CNB decreases as the stripping time increases (Fig. S2C). After 120 min, the CNS/CQDs yield is 20%. Fig S5 shows the TEM images of CNS/CQDs at different stripping times. The above results confirm that CNS/CQDs have been successfully obtained.

AFM further recorded the morphology and thickness of CNS/CQDs. The 3D topography shows that the CQDs are uniformly doped on the monolithic carbon nitride surface ( Fig. 6A). In addition, CNS/CQDs are not smooth, which can be further confirmed by the thickness fluctuations determined along the red and blue lines between the entire CNS/CQDs (Fig. 6B). In Fig. 6C-D, except for the edges, the thickness of the nanosheets ranges from 0.35 to 1.25 nm. It is confirmed that the layered structure of CNS/CQDs will shorten the transfer distance of charges to the material surface during photocatalysis [28]. Particle analysis of CNS/CQDs shows that the diameter of the CQDs is 24.14 nm, which is consistent with the TEM results.

Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis shows that CNS/CQDs have a high specific surface area (116.862 m2 g−1), which is 5.57 times higher than that of CNB (20.987 m2 g−1). Compared with CNB, the gas adsorption isotherm of CNS/CQDs presents a reversible type II isotherm, indicating the existence of a large number of micropores ( Fig. 7 A). In addition, the obvious H3 hysteresis loop indicates that CNS/CQDs are mesoporous. The pore size distribution peak further confirms the multi-porous structure in CNS/CQDs, which is higher and wider than that of CNB (Fig. 7B). Fig. 7 C shows that the pore volume of CNS/CQDs increases significantly (0.200 cm3 g−1), which is 3.64 times that of CNB (0.055 cm3 g−1). The surface area of mesopores in CNS/CQDs is relatively large, accounting for 75.20% of the surface area (Fig. 7D). The mesopores can enhance the mass transfer effect of the target, which is conducive to the improvement of the photocatalytic performance [29].

From the UV-Vis DRS results ( Fig. 8), it can be seen that the absorption intensity of CNS/CQDs is reduced compared with CNB, but still higher than that of single CNS (Fig. 8 A). This transition is consistent with the low degree of condensation of tri-s-triazine units [30]. In addition, there is an absorption shoulder in the spectra of CNS/CQDs, which may be caused by the π→π * transition of the CC bond. This is consistent with the results of XPS C1s spectra. The edge of the absorption wavelength in CNB is about 462 nm. Compared with CNB, the absorption wavelength edge of CNS/CQDs is significantly blue-shifted by 41 nm. The band gap width of CNS/CQDs becomes 3.04 eV in Fig. 8B. There are two reasons for this larger band gap. One is the pores in CNS/CQDs could weaken the conjugated system of carbon nitride, the other is the quantum confinement effect [31]. The XPS valence band (VB) spectra shows that the VB potentials of CNS/CQDs and CNB are 1.39 and 2.33 eV, respectively (Fig. 8 C). The electronic band gap structure of the sample is schematically shown in Fig. 8D. The conduction band (CB) position of CNS/CQDs shifts to a more negative direction, which is conducive to the generation of superoxide anions by dissolved oxygen in the photocatalytic system.

The fluorescence emission spectra show that CNS/CQDs material has a dense and narrow emission peak at about 462 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CNS/CQDs is higher than that of CNS and lower than that of CNB ( Fig. 9 A). The reason is that CQDs can convert visible lights into shorter wavelengths, which may increase the generation of electron-hole pairs to improve photocatalytic activity [32,33]. On the other hand, CQDs can accept photogenerated electrons from CNS, thereby reducing the photoluminescent intensity of CNS/CQDs [34]. The fluorescence phenomena of CNB, CNS and CNS/CQDs samples are presented in the insert figure of Fig. 9 A. Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectrum is performed to further study the behavior of photoexcited charge carriers (Fig. 9B). Table S2 summarizes the changes in fluorescence decay. The fluorescence decay curve shows a deviation from the second-order exponential decay. Compared with CNB, CNS shows slower decay kinetics, which means that the charge carriers in CNS have a longer lifetime. The extended lifetime indicates an increase in electron-hole separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity. The average lifetime of CNS/CQDs (1.2854 μs) is much shorter than that of CNB (1.3951 μs) and CNS (1.4096 μs). It shows that CQDs can effectively capture photo-generated electrons, thereby promoting charge separation and transfer [35,36]. Fig. 9C shows the upconversion photoluminescent spectra of CNS/CQDs. Most scholars believe that the upconversion photoluminescent characteristics of CQDs should be attributed to the multiphoton activation process [37,38]. Fig. 9D is the upconversion photoluminescence spectra of CNB and CNS/CQDs under 800 nm femtosecond pulse laser excitation. In the upconversion fluorescence spectra, the emission wavelength of CNS/CQDs is no different from that of CNB, but the bandwidth is much narrower than that of CNB. It is confirmed that the doping of CQDs could effectively solve the defects of low e--h+ separation rate and the insufficient utilization of visible light of carbon nitride.

The photocatalytic activities of different materials on Mel were studied. As shown in Fig. 10A, after 30 min of dark adsorption to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the adsorption capacity of CNS/CQDs on Mel is only 7.79%. The adsorption capacity of CNB and CNS samples can be ignored. Zeta potential analysis shows that CNS/CQDs catalyst has a stable negative potential (ZP = −45.2 mV) at pH = 6.5 (Fig. S6). Mel has an acidity coefficient pKa = 4.08, which can ionize colorless cations. Therefore, the negative charge on the surface of the CNS/CQDs could adsorb Mel through electrostatic attraction. As the electron withdrawing group, CQDs can not only enhance the transfer of photo-generated electrons, but also increase the adsorption capacity of Mel to some extent. Compared with CNB (24.90%) and CNS (27.96%), CNS/CQDs can efficiently degrade Mel within 45 min, with a degradation efficiency of 96.73%.

The pH value of the catalytic system is an important factor in practical application. In this study，the pH value not only changes the structure of Mel, but also affects the production and existence of·O2 -, thus to further affect the photocatalytic efficiency. When the pH is 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and without adjusted (pH = 13), the Mel removal rate is 96.60%, 90.71%, 88.84%, 91.50%, 98.25% and 89.88%, respectively (Fig. 10B). The reason is that the surface electronegativity of CNS/CQDs decreases with the increase of pH (Fig. S6). Dissolved oxygen is more likely to be adsorbed on the catalyst surface with low electronegativity, which can promote the production of·O2 -. The high concentration of H+ will become the capture agent of photogenerated electrons (Eq. 1) and inhibit the generation of superoxide anion free radicals. Therefore, the proper condition is pH = 11. In addition, the Mel removal rates of CNS/CQDs at pH = 3–13 are all over 88.00%, indicating that the applicable pH range is very wide.
(1)
H
+
+
e
−
→
H
2

Fig. 10 C shows the degradation effect of CNS/CQDs on different Mel concentrations. The zero-order (Eq. (2)), primary (Eq. (3)) and secondary (Eq. (4)) degradation kinetics of CNS/CQDs were studied respectively. The reaction equations are as follows:
(2)
C t=C 0-k 0 t
(3) ln(C t)=lnC 0-k 1 t
(4) 1/C t=1/C 0+k 2 t

Where t (min) is the reaction time; C t (mmol L−1) is the concentration of Mel at a certain reaction time; k 0 (mmol−1 L min−1), k 1 (min−1) and k 2 (mmol−1 L min−1) are the apparent rate constants of zero-order, first-order, and second-order reactions, respectively. As shown in Table 1, the regression coefficients (R 2) for the zero-order, first-order, and second-order reaction kinetics are 0.7262, 0.9539 and 0.8903, respectively. The degradation of Mel by CNS/CQDs conforms to the first-order reaction kinetics. When the concentration is 5 mg L−1, the first-order reaction kinetics constant k is 0.2045 min−1, and the half-life t 1/2 is only 5.31 min ( Table 2). When the concentration increases to 10 mg L−1, the k is 0.1135 min−1 and the t 1/2 is only 5.21 min. When the concentration exceeds 10 mg L−1, the degradation performance is significantly reduced. For example, at the concentration of 30 mg L−1, the t 1/2 reaches 19.62 min

The active sites of CNS/CQDs also affect Mel degradation (Fig. 10D). When the catalyst amount is low (5, 10, 15 mg), the active sites of the catalyst are insufficient, resulting in the slower oxidation reaction and lower removal rate. When the catalyst amount is 20 mg, the Mel removal rate can reach 99.60%. With the continuous increase in the amount of catalyst, the catalytic activity declines gradually. Under the condition of high CNS/CQDs amount, although the catalyst has more active sites, it will hinder the transmission of light and reduce the photocatalytic efficiency. In the actual photocatalysis process, the above parameters should be adjusted to effectively control the reaction rate.

Considering the complexity of water quality in the actual water treatment process, the comparative experiments of different water matrices were conducted. As seen in Fig. 11 A, the Mel removal rate is 99.98% (river water), 99.60% (deionized water) and 99.99% (tap water), respectively, which shows that CNS/CQDs catalyst has a strong anti-interference ability to water quality and is expected to be applied in practical treatment. Fig. 11B shows that CNS/CQDs catalyst has excellent stability and recyclability. After five successive photocatalytic degradation experiments, the Mel degradation efficiency only loses 1.2%. XRD analysis further verifies the chemical stability of CNS/CQDs (Fig. S7). Fig. 11 C shows the changes of TOC removal rate of Mel solution in the photocatalytic process. After 60 min, the TOC removal rate reaches 44.98%, showing good mineralization ability. In order to study the main active substances in Mel degradation process, Benzoquinone (BQ), Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were used as the scavengers for·O2 -, h+ and·OH, respectively [39,40]. Compared with no scavenger, adding 1 mM BQ, EDTA-2Na and DMSO to the reaction system, the degradation rates of Mel reach 34.24%, 96.18% and 96.59%, respectively (Fig. 11D). It shows that·O2 - is an important active substance in the photocatalytic reaction system.

Based on the above results and analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism of CNS/CQDs on Mel is shown in Fig. 12 . Under visible light irradiation, CNS can absorb the incident photon (
Vis
λ ≤ 462 nm
) and excited to produce e--h+. The generated h+ stayed in VB, while light-excited electrons (e-) migrated to CB. The photogenerated electrons in CB capture and reduce the molecular oxygen (O2) to produce·O2 - [41]. The upconversion characteristics of CQDs could convert
Vis
λ > 462 nm
into
Vis
λ ≤ 462 nm
, which improves the visible light utilization rate of material. Moreover, the doping of CQDs can store the energy, which enhances the transfer and separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and promotes the efficiency of·O2 - generation [42]. Subsequently,·O2 - quickly transfer to the surface of the photocatalyst and react with Mel [43]. In addition, many hydrophilic functional groups, such as carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs will promote the high dispersibility of CNS/CQDs in aqueous media [44]. CNS/CQDS can also improve the adsorption of Mel molecules through electrostatic attraction and the electron-withdrawing ability of CQDs. All these favorable factors help to improve the photocatalytic activity of CNS/CQDs.

In this study, CNS/CQDs material was synthesized by an in-situ one-step method for the first time and applied to the efficient degradation of Mel in aqueous solution under visible light. The thickness of the synthesized CNS/CQDs nanosheets ranged from 0.35 to 1.25 nm and the CQDs were uniformly doped on the surface of CNS. The introduction of CQDs could effectively promote the separation of electron-hole pair and the utilization of visible light. The CNS/CQDs showed a more efficient in Mel degradation than CNB and CNS, which could be ascribed to the energy storage and upconversion photoluminescent characteristics of CQDs. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of CNS/CQDs remained an excellent stability after reusing five times. This research would provide valuable guidance to develop the stable CQDs-based photocatalytic materials in the future.

Yongqiang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing − original draft preparation. Mengdan Zhang: Data curation, Validation. Jiamei Zhao: Visualization, Investigation. Chen Chen: Supervision. Yingying Zhou: Software, Validation. Xin Zheng: Investigation, Resources. Conglu Zhang: Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific Research Fund Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province, China(LJKZ0910) and the Career Development Support Plan for Young and Middle −aged Teachers in Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, China (ZQN2021026). The author thanks Dr. Guo Dong (Beijing Normal University) for his help in characterization analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129042.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
